evidences.content: File a Nominating Petition with the City Clerk containing 1,000 valid signatures (and no ﬁling fee) or 500 valid signatures (and a $300 Filing fee). File campaign disclosure statements. Attend campaign ﬁnance training. Disclose your ﬁnancial interests. • • • • • • WHAT ARE THE ETHICS REQUIREMENTS? The City of Los Angeles’ Ethics Commission requires you to: WHAT ELSE IS THERE TO KNOW? Filing your Declaration of Intention to Become a Candidate is the fastest, easiest part of the process. Gathering signatures on your Nominating Petitions requires signiﬁcant time, eﬀort, and attention to detail. [...] The more you know about what you need to do, the better your chances at success. To help you succeed, the City Clerk - Election Division is committed to providing tools to help you by: • • Oﬀering Candidate Filing and Petition Circulation Workshops. Linking you to a variety of helpful resources. Contact the Ethics Commission at (213) 978-1960, or visit their website at ethics.lacity.org for more information. • Reside in the City of Los Angeles if running for Mayor, City Attorney, or City Controller. evidences.title: Los Angeles mayor race: Full list of 14 candidates in 2026 election, Republicans and Democrats, hoping to replace Karen Bass - ABC7 Los Angeles
evidences.content: ## Juanita Lopez According to her campaign website, political scientist Juanita Lopez is running for mayor with a "commitment to bringing fresh perspectives and effective solutions to our city's most pressing challenges." "My experience at the Federal Reserve Bank involved managing multi-million dollar accounts with a strong emphasis on accountability," her bio on her website reads. "I will prioritize transparency and uphold ethical standards for the people." Her campaign is focused on community empowerment, economic transparency and sustainable growth with a cleaner environment for all. If elected, she also promises to tackle issues such as reinstating the "no-littering law" and reducing the sales tax to 8%. ## Adam Miller [...] "Asaad Alnajjars 'Engineering a Stronger Los Angeles' platform replaces political posturing with technical precision. His plan centers on a 90-day fiscal audit, an engineering-led infrastructure overhaul, and a housing-first approach to homelessness. By integrating digital twin technology for traffic and restoring animal shelter funding, Alnajjar aims to modernize city operations while maintaining a 'Unity through Diversity' framework that protects comm...

evidences.title: Here are the front runners for LA’s mayoral race
evidences.content: ### Adam Miller Adam Miller is an entrepreneur and CEO of the non-profit Better Angels. Miller made his wealth developing employee training software. Since then, Miller and his wife have since turned to philanthropic efforts to end homelessness in LA through his nonprofit Better Angels. Miller told The Los Angeles Times that city hall needs better management and that he believes he can provide that strong leadership. Miller’s platform doesn’t specifically state any policies or legislative experience, but he uses the pithy acronym THRIVES to describe a plan centered on housing affordability, providing job opportunities and updating first responder systems. ### Spencer Pratt [...] ### Nithya Raman LA City Councilmember Nithya Raman’s entry into local politics in 2020, supported by the Democratic Socialists of America, sparked a wave of progressives (Hugo Soto-Martínez, Eunisses Hernandez and Ysabel Jurado) voted into City Hall. She currently represents the council’s fourth district, a swath of LA that stretches from Reseda to Silver Lake. Raman has helped to strengthen renter protections by voting to cap rent increases and establishing “just cause” eviction protections. She also h...

evidences.title: Poll shows Bass, Raman and Pratt in tight race for mayor - Los Angeles Times
evidences.content: The poll also showed that in a head-to-head runoff between Bass and Raman, the councilmember would lead, 32% to 28%, among the city’s registered voters, but in this scenario, a quarter of likely voters say they would choose neither or would not vote, and 15% were undecided. The survey of 1,913 registered voters — 1,351 of whom are considered likely voters — is the largest sample of any public poll released in advance of the election. It was conducted between May 19 and 24. The poll has a margin of error of around 3% in either direction. Just 10% of voters were still undecided, the poll found, down from 26% when the last survey by Berkeley IGS was conducted March 9-15. Advertisement Mayoral candidate Spencer Pratt at a campaign block party in South Los Angeles last week. [...] Although Pratt now appears to have a chance at making the runoff, the poll showed he would face a steeper climb in potential November runoff scenarios with Bass or Raman. Pratt, a Republican who has been labeled “Trumpian” by Raman, is competing in a city where GOP registration is less than 15%. “Pratt is an unusual candidate and is generating a lot of enthusiasm in the primary, but he trails by double digi...

evidences.title: Will Someone Win the LA Mayor Race Outright in June?
evidences.content: Total volume of $1,232 reflects limited new conviction on either side. The data favors NO with near-consensus weight. The 7 percent YES price is residual insurance against an improbable consolidation, not a genuine forecast of majority victory. LINES VERDICT Runoff Inevitable Los Angeles has not seen a first-round outright winner in an open mayoral race in nearly a century, and a 14-candidate field with three leaders each below 25 percent makes June 2, 2026 no different. The polling math and the historical record point the same direction: November. [...] Total volume stands at $1,232, matching the 24-hour figure, against $24,587 in available liquidity. The order book outpaces trading activity by roughly 20-to-1. New capital sees no edge challenging the 93 percent consensus. ## Lines Analysis: Los Angeles and the Runoff Default Here’s what the market is missing: nothing. Bass leads at 22 percent. Pratt at 18 and Raman at 16 each hold enough support to block any majority. All three candidates are active and funded. The 93 percent NO pricing is pure arithmetic, not pessimism. A first-round Bass win requires both Pratt and Raman to exit or collapse before June 2. Neither has signale...

evidences.title: Los Angeles Mayor Primary Election Results
evidences.content: The market rules are for the eventual winner of the mayoral election, so unless one candidate gets more than 50% in the primary, the resolution will not be known until November. If the selected candidate is elected mayor in 2026, the market will resolve to Yes. The City of Los Angeles will verify the outcome. Get a detailed breakdown of the election betting odds for the LA Mayoral primary below. Sign up for our daily newsletter so you never miss a beat on all things federal ## Who Will Win the Los Angeles Mayoral Election? Despite the tight polls, Bass is widely expected to win the primary. evidences.content: 7. Election Timeline and Key Milestones Understanding the election schedule is critical for timing your campaign activities. Los Angeles municipal elections are now aligned with California state election dates, and the mayoral race uses a two-round system (primary and general): Keep an eye on any changes to election law. In recent years, L.A. adjusted its election calendar to line up with state elections to boost turnout . Also, deadlines sometimes shift slightly if state laws change or if there are holidays/weekends involved. The City Clerk’s Election Division publishes an official election calendar each cycle – use it as your bible for dates. evidences.content: #### Register by May 18 to Receive a Vote by Mail ballot; In-person Voting Begins May 23. LOS ANGELES — Los Angeles County Registrar-Recorder/County Clerk (RR/CC) Dean C. Logan reminds eligible voters that Monday,May 18, is the deadline to register to vote and receive a Vote by Mail ballotfor the June 2, 2026 Statewide Direct Primary Election. Registering to vote is quick and easy and can be completed online at LAVOTE.GOV. Voter registration applications are also available at most government buildings, such as libraries, the Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV), and RR/CC offices throughout Los Angeles County. Voters can check their voter registration to ensure they are registered to vote, and their information is up to date. [...] If eligible voters miss the deadline, they will not be issued a Vote by Mail ballot but can still vote in person at any Vote Center in Los Angeles County beginning Saturday, May 23. Voters displaced by wildfires do not need to change their registration and may add a temporary mailing address to receive election materials while away from home. evidences.title: Los Angeles, California - Ballotpedia
evidences.content: ### Mayor The mayor is the city's chief executive. The responsibilities of the mayor include proposing a budget, signing legislation into law, appointing departmental directors and committee members, and overseeing the city's day-to-day operations. The mayor also possesses veto powers. The current Mayor of Los Angeles is Karen Bass (nonpartisan). Bass assumed office in 2022. ### City council The Los Angeles City Council is the city's primary legislative body. It is responsible for adopting the city budget, approving mayoral appointees, overseeing the use of municipal properties, levying taxes, and making or amending city laws, policies, and ordinances. The city council is made up of 15 members. They are elected from 15 districts to four-year terms. [...] Seal of Los Angeles.png Mayor of Los Angeles Karen Bass Nonpartisan Assumed office: December 12, 2022 Los Angeles is a city in Los Angeles County, California. evidences.content: that the candidate’s campaign shall “…not be liable for any violations resulting from the reproduction if it provides sufficient evidence to show that it had no prior knowledge of the reproduction.”(#_ftn52) The Los Angeles City Ethics Commission issued penalties for a violation of this rule in 2015, when it found that a third party spent money in excess of the contribution limit to reproduce and distribute a “…two-page mailer…[that was] almost identical to a mailer previously created and distributed by the candidate committee…”.(#_ftn53) The Los Angeles Ethics Commission did not need to prove direct coordination between the candidate and the third-party spender in order to hold the third-party spender liable for spending money to republish the candidate’s mailer; the republication alone [...] Los Angeles: The City of Los Angeles has a rule specifying that the reproduction of campaign materials constitutes an in-kind contribution to the candidate whose materials are being reproduced. evidences.title: L.A.'s Broken Elections - New America
evidences.content: L.A. requires runoff elections to ensure that a candidate is elected with a majority of the vote. Historically, that has meant two separate elections held months apart. The problem, of course, is that each election costs something in the neighborhood of $8 million and voter participation drops precipitously for the second election. In 2007, for example, only six percent of voters participated in L.A.’s runoff election. By using Instant Runoff Voting, L.A. could combine two rounds of elections into one, saving millions of dollars and increasing voter turnout in the process. [...] Instead of running a seemingly endless series of expensive elections that the city can’t afford and no one shows up for, how about trying something different? That something different is Instant Runoff Voting. L.A. requires runoff elections to ensure that a candidate is elected with a majority of the vote. Historically, that has meant two separate elections held months apart. The problem, of course, is that each election costs something in the neighborhood of $8 million and voter participation drops precipitously for the second election. In 2007, for example, only six percent of voters participated in L....
